saida_bruta:
"Your face looks familiar," he remarked politely. "Weren't you with the First Division during the war?"

"Yes, I was. I served in the Twenty-eighth Infantry."

"I was in the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I felt sure I'd seen you somewhere before."

We chatted briefly about a few damp, grey little villages in France. He clearly lived nearby, as he mentioned he had recently purchased a hydroplane and planned to test it the following morning.

"Would you like to join me, old sport? Just a quick run along the shore of the Sound."

"What time?"

"Whatever time works best for you."

I was just about to ask his name when Jordan turned toward us with a smile.

"Are you having a good time now?" she asked.

"Much better." I turned back to my new companion. "This is quite an unusual party for me. I haven't even met the host yet. I live right over there—" I gestured toward the distant, hidden hedge, "and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an invitation."

He stared at me for a moment, looking thoroughly puzzled.

"I'm Gatsby," he said abruptly.

"What!" I gasped. "Oh, please forgive me."

"I assumed you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm not a very attentive host."

saida_bruta:
"Your face looks familiar," he remarked politely. "Weren't you in the First Division during the war?"

"Why, yes. I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry."

"I was with the Sixteenth until June of nineteen-eighteen. I knew I'd seen you somewhere before."

We talked for a moment about some wet, grey little French villages. We must have lived near each other, for he told me he had just bought a hydroplane and was going to try it out in the morning.

"Want to go with me, old sport? Just near the shore along the Sound."

"What time?"

"Any time that suits you best."

I was on the point of asking his name when Jordan look around and smiled.

"Having a better time now?" she inquired.

"Much better." I turned again to my new acquaintance. "This is an unusual party for me. I haven't even seen the host. I live over there—" I waved my hand at the invisible hedge in the distance, "and this man Gatsby sent over his chauffeur with an invitation."

For a moment he looked at me as if he failed to understand.

"I'm Gatsby," he said suddenly.

"What!" I exclaimed. "Oh, I beg your pardon."

"I thought you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm not a very good host."

"Your face looks familiar," he remarked politely. "Weren't you in the Third Division during the war?"

"Why, yes. I was in the Ninth Machine-gun Battalion."

"I was in the Seventh Infantry myself until June of nineteen-eighteen. I knew I'd seen you somewhere before."

We talked for a moment about some wet, grey little villages in France. We must have lived near each other, for he told me he had just bought a hydroplane and was going to try it out in the morning.

saida_bruta:
"Your face seems familiar," he observed politely. "Weren't you with the Third Division in the war?"

"Yes, indeed. I was in the Ninth Machine-gun Battalion."

"I served with the Seventh Infantry until June of nineteen-eighteen. I felt sure I had came across you somewhere."

We chatted briefly about those damp, grey little French villages. It turned out we had lived close by, and he mentioned he had recently purchased a hydroplane, which he planned to test the following morning.

"Would you care to join me, old sport? Just a quick run along the Sound near the shore."

"At what time?"

"Whenever it suits you best."

I was about to ask his name when Jordan turned around and offered a smile.

"Are you enjoying yourself more now?" she asked.

"Much more." I turned back to my new companion. "This is quite an unusual gathering for me. I haven't even met the host yet. I live just over there—" I gestured toward the distant, hidden hedge, "and this fellow Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an invitation."

He stared at me for a second, looking completely puzzled.

"I'm Gatsby," he revealed suddenly.

"What!" I gasped. "Oh, please forgive me."

"I assumed you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm rather poor at hosting."

We talked for a moment about some wet, gray little French villages. It turned out we had lived quite close together, and he mentioned he had recently bought a hydroplane and was going to try it out tomorrow morning.

"Want to go with me, old sport? Just near the shore along the Sound."

"What time?"

"Any time that suits you best."

I was just about to ask his name when Jordan walked over and smiled.

"Having a better time now?" she inquired.

"Much better." I turned back to my companion. "This is an unusual party for me. I haven't even met the host yet. I live right over there—" I waved my hand at the invisible hedge in the distance, "and this man Gatsby sent his chauffeur over with an invitation."

For a moment he looked at me in absolute amazement.

"I'm Gatsby," he said suddenly.

"What!" I exclaimed. "Oh, I beg your pardon."

"I thought you knew, old sport. I'm afraid I'm not a very good host.

"Yes, I was. I served in the Ninth Machine-gun Battalion."
